teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the programmer's question: why did the robot move that way?
teaching_arc.progression: Move from coordinate frames to motion semantics to termination behavior.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish with a stronger mental model for reading and writing motion instructions.

audience_level: undergraduate industrial robotics students preparing for labs, robot selection, and basic programming decisions
topic_goal: Help students understand how coordinate choices, motion commands, and termination settings shape actual robot movement.
topic_takeaways: Students should distinguish common coordinate systems used in robot programming.
Students should understand what motion termination changes physically.
Students should be able to predict when a motion command will look smooth, slow, or precise.
style_notes: Keep the tone practical and shop-floor oriented.
Translate definitions into robot-cell decisions quickly.
Prefer concrete industrial examples over abstract theory when possible.
Use movement language students can picture physically.
Make the connection between programming terms and visible robot behavior explicit.
transition_style: Move from the industrial question students would ask on the job to the next specification, control, or programming choice they need to make.

slides.narration_seed: Tell students: this topic is the foundation of safe programming. If you choose the wrong coordinate system or motion type, the robot can move in a completely unexpected way.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Help students understand how coordinate choices, motion commands, and termination settings shape actual robot movement.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Why Coordinate Systems Matter so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Frame Coordinates + Motion Systems as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep Coordinates + Motion Systems connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Coordinates + Motion Systems sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slides.narration_seed: Emphasize: frame = an origin + 3 axes. The robot’s controller always needs a frame to interpret X/Y/Z and W/P/R.
must_cover: They provide reference frames for positioning and orienting the robot’s tool (end effector).
The same jog key can produce different motion depending on your selected coordinate system.
Coordinate systems help you program motion relative to the robot, relative to the cell, or relative to the tool.
Main coordinate systems we use today: WORLD, JOINT, TOOL.
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Coordinates + Motion Systems into Why Coordinate Systems Matter so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into One Robot, Multiple Frames so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Use Why Coordinate Systems Matter to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep Why Coordinate Systems Matter connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Why Coordinate Systems Matter sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slides.narration_seed: Explain: WORLD is like Earth coordinates in the cell; USER is like coordinates on the table; TOOL is like coordinates attached to the gripper. The robot can show the same pose using any of these frames.
must_cover: WORLD frame: fixed to the robot base / cell.
USER frame: fixed to your table/fixture/workpiece.
TOOL frame: fixed to the end effector (TCP).
When you jog, you are telling FANUC: “Move the TCP along this frame’s X/Y/Z.”
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into JOINT Coordinate System so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for One Robot, Multiple Frames first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep One Robot, Multiple Frames connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture One Robot, Multiple Frames clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slides.narration_seed: Joint mode is great for ‘freeing’ the robot or adjusting posture, but it is NOT intuitive for moving the tool straight. Warn: the TCP can swing widely even when you move only one joint.
must_cover: Definition: Each axis of the robot is controlled independently.
Use Case: Direct control of individual robot joints.
Example: Maintenance or calibration tasks.
Robot position is shown using joint angles (J1…J6).
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into CARTESIAN / WORLD Coordinate System so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for JOINT Coordinate System first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep JOINT Coordinate System connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture JOINT Coordinate System clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slides.narration_seed: Quick WPR explanation: W≈roll, P≈pitch, R≈yaw (don’t overdo the math). Emphasize: WORLD jog means “move along the cell axes,” not along the tool axes.
must_cover: Definition: A fixed coordinate system used for absolute positioning in the robot workspace.
Origin: Located at the base of the robot (cell/world reference).
Axes: X (forward), Y (left), Z (up).
Moves the robot Tool Center Point (TCP) in X/Y/Z and rotates about X (W), Y (P), Z (R).
slides.transition_from_previous: Use CARTESIAN / WORLD Coordinate System to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once CARTESIAN / WORLD Coordinate System is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Define CARTESIAN / WORLD Coordinate System in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep CARTESIAN / WORLD Coordinate System connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain CARTESIAN / WORLD Coordinate System accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slides.narration_seed: Tell students: USER frames are one of the most important productivity tools. When your part is on a rotated fixture, USER frame lets you program in part coordinates.
must_cover: WORLD: Fixed to the robot base/cell. Good for general navigation.
USER: Fixed to a workpiece/table/fixture. Best for programming relative to the part.
JGFRAME (JOG FRAME): A jog-only frame used to make manual jogging convenient in certain setups.
In practice: If your table is rotated, USER frame prevents confusing diagonal moves.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into TOOL Coordinate System so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for WORLD → WORLD / USER / JGFRAME first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep WORLD → WORLD / USER / JGFRAME connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture WORLD → WORLD / USER / JGFRAME clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slides.narration_seed: If your TCP is wrong, tool jogging is lying to you. Example: drilling—Tool +Z should move along the drill direction even if angled.
must_cover: Definition: A coordinate system attached to the robot’s end effector or tool.
Use Case: Used when programming movements relative to the tool’s orientation.
Example: Welding, painting, drilling—where the tool angle matters.
Moves the robot TCP in X/Y/Z and rotates W/P/R in the selected tool frame.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Pose = Position + Orientation so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for TOOL Coordinate System first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep TOOL Coordinate System connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture TOOL Coordinate System clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slides.narration_seed: Do a quick demo idea: same XYZ different WPR—students can visually see the wrist rotate. Stress: pose must be safe; a wrist flip can hit fixtures.
must_cover: Position: X, Y, Z (usually in mm).
Orientation: W, P, R (tool rotation angles shown by FANUC).
If you keep X/Y/Z the same but change W/P/R, the tool will stay at the same location but rotate.
This is why wrist joints (J4–J6) matter: they control orientation.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Pose = Position + Orientation to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Pose = Position + Orientation is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Pose = Position + Orientation in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Pose = Position + Orientation connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Pose = Position + Orientation accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Motion type answers ‘How do we travel between points?’ Termination answers ‘What happens as we reach the point?’ Students often mix these up.
must_cover: The four types are: JOINT, LINEAR, CIRCULAR, ARC.
Understanding differences is crucial for precise positioning, smooth motion, and optimized cycle times.
Think: motion type controls the path between points.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Motion Types in FANUC to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Motion Types in FANUC is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Motion Types in FANUC in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Motion Types in FANUC connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Motion Types in FANUC accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Explain: Joint motion optimizes joints, not tool path. Good for ‘air moves.’ Bad when you need a straight approach to a part.
must_cover: Moves the robot using the shortest path in joint space.
Fastest movement between points where path accuracy is not critical.
Command example: J P[x] 100% FINE
Pros: Fast and efficient.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into LINEAR (L) Motion so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think URDF, Xacro, links, and joints are just file syntax instead of a robot-structure model.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for JOINT (J) Motion first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep JOINT (J) Motion connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture JOINT (J) Motion clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Linear motion is the default near the part. If the robot must approach a hole or fixture safely, L is your friend.
must_cover: Moves the robot in a straight line from current position to the target (TCP path).
Used when you need precise path control (welding, gluing, approach/withdraw).
Command example: L P[x] 500mm/sec FINE
Pros: Maintains a straight path.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into CIRCULAR (C) Motion so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for LINEAR (L) Motion first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep LINEAR (L) Motion connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture LINEAR (L) Motion clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Explain: Typically you need a ‘through’ point and an ‘end’ point (implementation depends on setup). Emphasize: circular is about TCP path being an arc.
must_cover: Moves the robot in a circular arc defined by points.
Used for curved paths (polishing, contour following, some welding paths).
Command example: C P[x] P[y] 100mm/sec FINE
Pros: Smooth, curved motion.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into ARC Motion (Welding-Oriented) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for CIRCULAR (C) Motion first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep CIRCULAR (C) Motion connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture CIRCULAR (C) Motion clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Tell students: We may not use ARCPATH in this course unless the cell has the welding option. But you should know it exists and why it is different.
must_cover: Specialized motion for arc welding, maintaining consistent speed and orientation.
Used in welding applications (requires appropriate options/software).
Command mentioned in the original slides: ARCPATH
Pros: Optimized for weld quality and process consistency.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use ARC Motion (Welding-Oriented) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once ARC Motion (Welding-Oriented) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define ARC Motion (Welding-Oriented) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep ARC Motion (Welding-Oriented) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain ARC Motion (Welding-Oriented) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: Speed Units: % vs mm/sec vs cm/min

slides.narration_seed: Students get confused by units. Make it explicit: J% is not the same as mm/sec.
must_cover: Joint (J) commonly uses % of maximum speed (example: J P[1] 100% ...).
Linear (L) often uses mm/sec or cm/min (examples: 500mm/sec, 1000cm/min).
Circular (C) often uses linear-style units too (example: 100mm/sec).
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Speed is independent from termination: you can have fast FINE or slow CNT depending on requirements.
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from ARC Motion (Welding-Oriented) into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Speed Units: % vs mm/sec vs cm/min explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Speed Units: % vs mm/sec vs cm/min and know which side fits which situation.

slides.narration_seed: Point out: P[] is a taught point stored in the program; PR[] is a variable you can modify. Explain UF/UT briefly: the same XYZ means different physical locations depending on the active frames.
must_cover: 1) Motion format: J, L, C
2) Target position: P[i] (taught point) or PR[i] (position register)
3) Feedrate: J% or mm/sec or cm/min (depends on motion type)
4) Path/termination: FINE or CNT0…CNT100
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Inside a Point: UF/UT + XYZ + WPR + CONF so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Program Line Anatomy: Motion Instruction first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Program Line Anatomy: Motion Instruction connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Program Line Anatomy: Motion Instruction clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slide_ref.source_title: Inside a Point: UF/UT + XYZ + WPR + CONF

slides.narration_seed: Explain gently: you don’t need to memorize CONF fields today, but you must respect them. If the robot ‘flips’ the wrist unexpectedly, configuration is often involved.
must_cover: UF: n = active User Frame number at the time of teaching.
UT: n = active Tool Frame number at the time of teaching.
X, Y, Z = TCP position (typically mm).
W, P, R = TCP orientation angles displayed by FANUC.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Inside a Point: UF/UT + XYZ + WPR + CONF to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Inside a Point: UF/UT + XYZ + WPR + CONF is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Inside a Point: UF/UT + XYZ + WPR + CONF in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Inside a Point: UF/UT + XYZ + WPR + CONF connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Inside a Point: UF/UT + XYZ + WPR + CONF accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: Reading the Teach Pendant Program Screen

slides.narration_seed: Tell students: troubleshooting starts here. If it won’t run, check mode, check alarms, check status, check override, check where the cursor is, and check if FWD is disabled.
must_cover: Program being executed vs program being edited.
Line number and current line (where the cursor is).
Execution status: ABORTED / PAUSED / RUNNING.
Jog type shown (e.g., JOINT).
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Quiz: Coordinate Systems so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Reading the Teach Pendant Program Screen first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Reading the Teach Pendant Program Screen connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Reading the Teach Pendant Program Screen clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: After answer: remind them USER is for part/table; WORLD is for cell; JOINT is motor-by-motor; TOOL is for tool-direction moves.
must_cover: You are holding a drill tool angled 30°. You want to jog straight forward along the drill direction (the tool’s own +Z). Which coordinate system should you use?
In TOOL coordinates, X/Y/Z axes are attached to the tool frame. That’s how you move along the tool direction even when it is angled.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: Coordinate Systems and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quiz: Coordinate Systems into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quiz: Coordinate Systems question on their own and explain the reasoning.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slides.narration_seed: Motion type = path between points. Termination = behavior at the point.
must_cover: Two most common termination types: FINE and CNT.
FINE: robot comes to a complete stop at the target before continuing.
CNT (0–100): robot blends the motion and does not fully stop at intermediate points.
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Understanding the difference improves accuracy, smoothness, and cycle time.
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Quiz: Coordinate Systems into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Motion Termination: FINE vs CNT explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Motion Termination: FINE vs CNT and know which side fits which situation.

slides.narration_seed: Explain: FINE is like ‘hit the exact point.’ Downside: stopping costs time and can cause jerky motion. Use FINE where it matters most—near parts and fixtures.
must_cover: FINE forces the robot to come to a complete stop at the target position before executing the next instruction.
Ensures maximum accuracy; used when exact positioning is critical.
Common uses: pick-and-place, assembly, vision-guided operations, stopping at a sensor/fixture.
Example motion line: L P[2] 500mm/sec FINE
must_say: Repeatability is consistency. Accuracy is closeness to the intended target.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into FINE: Advantages vs Disadvantages so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often collapse accuracy, repeatability, and precision into one vague idea.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for FINE (Precision Stopping) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep FINE (Precision Stopping) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture FINE (Precision Stopping) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Instructor tip: tell them not to default to FINE everywhere. Use it strategically—final approach and interaction points.
must_cover: Advantages of FINE
• Ensures precise stopping at the target point.
• Essential for operations requiring exact positioning.
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
• Required when interacting with fixtures, sensors, vision systems, or precise assembly points.
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from FINE (Precision Stopping) into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on FINE: Advantages vs Disadvantages explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of FINE: Advantages vs Disadvantages and know which side fits which situation.

slides.narration_seed: Explain CNT numbers: bigger = more corner rounding/blending (less exact at the point). Practical rule: CNT for motion efficiency; FINE for precision events.
must_cover: CNT allows blending by not coming to a complete stop at each programmed point.
Robot starts moving toward the next position before fully reaching the current one (smooth transition).
CNT value (0–100) determines how much blending occurs.
Common use: travel moves, smooth scanning/painting paths, material handling.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into CNT: Advantages vs Disadvantages so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for CNT (Continuous / Blended Motion) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep CNT (Continuous / Blended Motion) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture CNT (Continuous / Blended Motion) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Add a warning: if a student uses CNT at a ‘touch’ point (sensor/fixture), the robot might never truly reach it. That’s a common beginner mistake.
must_cover: Advantages of CNT
• Improves cycle time by eliminating unnecessary stops.
• Produces smoother motion between waypoints.
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
• Reduces mechanical stress on robot joints by avoiding sudden stops.
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from CNT (Continuous / Blended Motion) into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on CNT: Advantages vs Disadvantages explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of CNT: Advantages vs Disadvantages and know which side fits which situation.

slide_ref.source_title: When to Use FINE vs CNT

slides.narration_seed: Tell students: ‘Near the part = FINE, in the air = CNT’ is a good beginner rule. Later they’ll learn to tune CNT values for smoothness vs accuracy.
must_cover: Precise positioning (pick & place, welding, assembly).
Stopping at a sensor or fixture.
Vision-guided alignment and measurement.
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Path-following (painting, scanning) → CNT (50–100)
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from CNT: Advantages vs Disadvantages into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on When to Use FINE vs CNT explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of When to Use FINE vs CNT and know which side fits which situation.

slides.narration_seed: After answer: ask them ‘When would you NOT use linear?’—example: long travel moves where path isn’t important.
must_cover: Which motion type guarantees the TCP follows a straight line from the current point to the target point?
Linear (L) is designed for a straight TCP path. Joint motion is fastest but the TCP path can curve.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: Motion Types and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Quiz: Motion Types without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quiz: Motion Types into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quiz: Motion Types question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slides.narration_seed: Reinforce: if they use CNT, the robot may round the corner and never truly reach the exact contact point.
must_cover: You must hit a fixture point exactly (touch a hard stop) before closing the gripper. Which termination should you choose?
FINE forces a full stop at the point, giving maximum positioning accuracy at that target.
Students should distinguish common coordinate systems used in robot programming.
Students should understand what motion termination changes physically.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Quiz: Termination to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
slides.transition_to_next: The next slide should compress the lesson into a quick memory or self-check.
slides.explanation_style: Use Quiz: Termination as a final self-check so students leave knowing what they should now be able to explain or do.
slides.story_hint: Keep Quiz: Termination connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave Quiz: Termination with the main takeaways of the topic compressed into usable memory.

slides.narration_seed: Close with a practical preview: next lab, students will (1) jog in different frames, (2) teach 2–3 points, (3) write J/L moves, (4) try FINE vs CNT and see the difference in smoothness and accuracy.
must_cover: 1) Coordinate system for jogging/teaching (JOINT, WORLD, USER, TOOL, JGFRAME).
2) Motion type (J, L, C, ARC/ARCPATH).
3) Speed (%, mm/sec, cm/min).
Students should distinguish common coordinate systems used in robot programming.
4) Termination (FINE vs CNT 0–100).
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Summary: The 4 Knobs You Control to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
slides.transition_to_next: Close by restating the destination of the topic: Students should finish with a stronger mental model for reading and writing motion instructions.
slides.explanation_style: Use Summary: The 4 Knobs You Control as a final self-check so students leave knowing what they should now be able to explain or do.
